# Transcrição: 06.1. Peaks & Valleys Method

- **Fonte:** 06.1. Peaks & Valleys Method
- **Duração:** 05:22
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Since the goal of gathering your stories is to come up with the most significant and impactful stories of your life, it's important to be able to identify which stories those are. If you're having a tough time deciding which stories are the most impactful, there's a method we often use that can help you make it more visual and obvious. It's called the peaks and valleys approach. Now, just to be clear, no matter how you go about gathering your stories, it's important to remember that you'll still enter the details of those stories within the online course. The peaks and valleys approach still draws on the same stories you've thought of, while at the same time helping you pick out and clearly identify which of those stories have had the most significance in your life, the best of the best and the worst of the worst. If you want to give it a go, here's how you go about it. Take out a blank sheet of paper and draw a horizontal line across the middle. Stories you put above the line are those you consider happy memories. Moments you'd be happy to relive. Stories below the line are events that you wouldn't necessarily want to relive, but have impacted your life and shaped who you are today. Write a few words to capture each story as you fill out the chart. The higher you plot the stories above the line, the more fulfilling and positive they were. The lower you plot the stories below the line, the more challenging or difficult they were. You'll probably end up with stories of various levels. Write as many stories as come to mind. Don't overthink it. If it was a story that carries impact and meaning, write it down. You may only share some of the stories you collected, and you may even tell a story or two that pops into your mind in the moment you're with your partner. All of that's okay. This is simply an exercise to get the juices flowing. As you gather your stories on paper, capture each one with a headline. You'll write the full version of a few of the stories in the course later, so for now, just write enough to jog your memory. When mapping your stories, write about specific experiences from your life, like a particular project you loved working on, a time you were doing something you felt called to do, or even a low point in your life that taught you an invaluable lesson. Your stories need to be specific. The more specific, the better. Choose the moments of real significance, those whether good or bad that you carry with you today. They can be big or small. They can be from as far back as you can remember up until yesterday. It all counts. Let me give you a specific example of one of my Valley stories, and Peter will give you a specific example of a peak story. Like many people, 2008 was a really rough year for me. I had started a business related to and dependent on the real estate market

## [03:01 - 05:22]

the year before when things were still going great. Then it all fell apart. I lost my business, my home, a rental property, and all but $800. My wife was pregnant with our first child, and I had just started MBA school. I was devastated. I remember lying in my bed one night, crying, completely unsure of what I was going to do to support my family. I wasn't a pretty bad place for a few months, and I just felt miserable. Then one day I realized that being a victim of my situation was only going to make things worse. In that moment, I learned a valuable lesson. But even though it might take some time to get there, I needed to start focusing on the future instead of the past. While I wouldn't ever want to go through that experience again, I also wouldn't trade it for the world because of what I learned from it. I learned the value of determination, grit, and gratitude. And I learned that moving forward, even a small step at a time, is what brings me a lot of fulfillment. Peter, you want to share one of your peak stories with us? Sure. I remember when I was maybe eight or nine years old, I used to help out an uncle of mine, Frank, who had great difficulty walking because of injuries he'd suffered as a raw marine. I used to help him get around and manage the household chores. There was this one particular time when I helped Frank with his grocery shopping. He drove us to the local town, and because it was difficult for him to get in out of his car, we actually drove down the pavement, that's what we call a sidewalk, and with Frank shouting his food orders out of the car window to the butchers and bakers as he went along. I would then run in and collect the meat and bread. I loved it. The fact that Frank had trouble walking didn't stop him at all. He had great character and made everyone smile. Remember, whether you use the memory prompts within the course, the peak and valley method you put down on paper, or your own method to gather your stories, make sure you enter titles and all the details in the online course.
